lesson.menu_desc: Q&A under pressure: clarifying, conceding the small point and standing by your core result -- 28 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 9 -- Defending Your Data Under Pressure
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Patricia Ruffo | Aula 9 -- Defending Your Data Under Pressure

listenings.text: Thank you for that challenging question. Let me clarify what the data actually shows. Our study followed three hundred patients over twelve months, and the main result was consistent across every site. Now, I want to recognize the point you raised about the control group. That is a fair concern, and we addressed it in the supplementary analysis. However, I have to stand by the central finding, because it held up under several different statistical tests. I understand that one single trial is never the whole story. That said, our result is robust, and it agrees with two earlier studies from independent teams. So while I am happy to concede that we need more long-term data, I am confident that the core conclusion will survive further scrutiny. I would be glad to send you the full dataset so you can review the methods yourself.

Hi Patricia, this is David from the review committee. I listened again to your answers during the Q and A, and overall I thought you handled the pressure very well. You did not get defensive, you clarified your points calmly, and you recognized the weaker parts of the study without losing the main argument. I do have one comment. When the reviewer called your evidence anecdotal, your rebuttal was strong, but it was a little too fast. Next time, take a breath, restate the question, and then address it point by point. That said, your data is robust, and you clearly stand by it for the right reasons. Keep that calm tone under scrutiny and you will be very convincing at the symposium. Talk soon.

extra_audio.text: First, recognize the question and thank the person for it. Next, clarify exactly what your data shows. After that, concede any fair and minor point. Then, give your rebuttal and stand by the central finding. Finally, offer to share the dataset for further scrutiny.
